headline: Shelter Vet Reveal The Truth…
body:
I left my cat with my neighbor for a month.

When I came back, I didn't recognize her.

Not because she looked different.

Because she acted like a completely different cat.

No vomiting. Shiny coat. Running around like a kitten.

For two weeks, I tried to figure out what my neighbor did while I was gone.

What I finally discovered made my jaw drop.

And made me realize my vet has been hiding something from me for years.

My name is Margaret Rose. I'm 46. I live in Austin, Texas.

And I need to tell you what happened because I think your vet is hiding the same thing from you.

Let me start from the beginning.

Two years ago, I adopted a cat named Bella from the shelter.

She was perfect. Beautiful. Sweet.

But she threw up constantly.

2-3 times a week. Every single week.

Sometimes it was food. Sometimes bile. Sometimes just foam.

I took her to the vet.

"It's hairballs," he said. "Totally normal."

He sold me hairball control treats. $35.

Bella still threw up.

I went back.

"Sensitive stomach," he said. "Let's try prescription food."

$85 a bag.

Bella still threw up 2-3 times a week.

I tried every food. Hill's. Royal Canin. Blue Buffalo. Purina.

Nothing worked.

For two years, I just accepted it.

"Cats throw up. It's what they do."

That's what my vet kept saying.

Then last summer, my husband surprised me with a month-long trip to Europe.

Paris. Rome. Barcelona.

I was so excited.

But then I realized: what do I do with Bella?

My mom couldn't watch her. She's allergic.

My friends were all busy.

Boarding was too expensive for a whole month.

Then I remembered my neighbor, Carol.

She lives two doors down. Mid-60s. Retired. Really sweet.

We'd talked a few times. She mentioned she loved cats.

I knocked on her door one afternoon.

"Hey Carol, this is kind of last minute, but would you be able to watch my cat for a month? I'll pay you, of course."

Her face lit up. "Oh, I'd love to! I miss having a cat around."

"I have to warn you though," I said. "Bella throws up a lot. 2-3 times a week. My vet says it's normal, but it's kind of gross. You'll have to clean it up."

Carol waved her hand. "Oh, don't worry about that. I'm sure she'll be fine."

I felt so relieved.

I dropped Bella off at Carol's house the day before we left.

Showed her where the food was. The litter box. Everything.

"Call me if there are any problems," I said.

"She'll be perfect," Carol said. "Have fun on your trip!"

We flew to Paris the next morning.

For the first week, I was so anxious.

I kept thinking about Bella throwing up all over Carol's house.

I texted Carol a few days in.

"How's Bella doing? Is she throwing up a lot?"

Carol texted back: "She's wonderful! No problems at all."

I figured she was just being nice.

Week two, I texted again.

"Seriously, if Bella's being a problem, let me know. I know the vomiting is annoying."

Carol: "She's perfect! Honestly, she's such a sweet girl. No issues."

Okay. Maybe Bella was behaving because she was in a new environment.

By week three, I stopped worrying.

Carol kept sending me pictures. Bella curled up on the couch. Bella looking out the window.

She looked... happy.

We got back from Europe four weeks later.

I drove straight to Carol's house to pick up Bella.

Carol opened the door with this huge smile.

"Margaret! How was the trip?"

"It was amazing. How was Bella?"

"Oh, she was wonderful. Come in, I'll get her."

I walked into Carol's living room.

And that's when I heard it.

Running.

Fast little paws on hardwood.

Bella came sprinting around the corner.

I almost didn't believe it was her.

Her coat was shiny. Glossy. Like she'd just come from the groomer.

She was running. Actually running.

I hadn't seen Bella run in months.

She jumped on the couch, looked at me, then jumped down and ran to the window.

Playing.

Bella never played.

"Oh my god," I said. "What did you do to her?"

Carol laughed. "Nothing! She's been like this the whole time."

"Did she throw up at all?"

Carol thought for a second. "Maybe once or twice? Early on when she first got here. But after that, no. She's been totally fine."

I stared at her.

"Once or twice? That's it?"

"Yeah. Why?"

"Carol, Bella throws up 2-3 times a week at my house. Every single week. For two years."

Carol's face changed. "Really?"

"Yes. My vet says it's normal. Hairballs and sensitive stomach."

She frowned. "Huh. Well, she definitely hasn't been throwing up here."

I didn't understand.

"What did you feed her?"

"Oh, just what you left. That big bag of food. And some wet food in the evenings."

Same food I always gave her.

"Did you do anything different? Different water? Different routine?"

Carol shrugged. "Not really. She just ate, slept, played. Normal cat stuff."

I took Bella home that day completely confused.

For three days, Bella was perfect.

No vomiting. Energetic. Playing with her toys.

Then on day four, she threw up.

Day six, she threw up again.

By the end of the first week back home, we were back to 2-3 times a week.

Her coat started looking dull again.

She stopped running around.

She went back to sleeping all day.

I felt like I was losing my mind.

What happened at Carol's house?

For two weeks, I obsessed over it.

I changed Bella's water bowl. Still threw up.

I changed her litter. Still threw up.

I moved her food to a different spot. Still threw up.

I tried feeding her at different times. Still threw up.

Nothing worked.

I posted in my Facebook cat group.

"My cat stayed with a neighbor for a month and didn't throw up at all. Now she's back home and throwing up 2-3 times a week again. What changed?"

Everyone had theories:

"Maybe she was stressed at home and relaxed at the neighbor's?"

"Could be something in your house. Cleaning products? Air freshener?"

"Try a calming collar."

I tried everything they suggested.

Bella kept throwing up.

By the third week, I was so frustrated I wanted to scream.

Bella had been perfect at Carol's house for an entire month.

And I couldn't figure out why.

Finally, I just gave up trying to figure it out myself.

I walked over to Carol's house and knocked on her door.

"Carol, I need to ask you something."

She opened the door. "Of course! Come in."

We sat in her living room.

"Bella's throwing up again," I said. "2-3 times a week. Just like before. But she was perfect at your house. I've tried everything to figure out what you did differently, and I can't. Please, what did you do?"

Carol looked at me for a moment.

"Margaret, I should probably tell you something I didn't mention before. I'm a retired veterinarian. I worked at animal shelters for 30 years."

My mouth fell open.

"You're a vet?"

"Was. I retired five years ago."

"Why didn't you tell me?"

She shrugged. "You didn't ask. And honestly, I didn't think it mattered. I was just watching your cat."

I leaned forward. "Carol, Bella was perfect at your house for a month. You're a vet. You had to have done something."

"Well," she said. "I did treat Bella the way I treated every cat at the shelter. It's just... automatic for me after 30 years. I don't even think about it anymore."

"What do you mean?"

"At the shelter, we had a protocol. Day one for every cat: check for fleas, make sure they're eating properly, and start them on taurine supplements."

"Taurine? What's that?"

"It's an amino acid. Cats can't make it on their own—they have to get it from food. We saw deficiency in almost every cat that came through our doors. So we just started giving it to everyone automatically."

I stared at her. "You gave Bella taurine?"

"Yes. It's standard protocol. Every single cat at the shelter got it because we saw chronic vomiting, lethargy, dull coats—all signs of deficiency—in probably 80% of the cats we took in."

"But Bella eats expensive food. My vet recommended it. It has everything she needs."

Carol shook her head. "The food might say it has taurine on the label. But most cats can't absorb it properly. Especially if the food has peas or lentils or chickpeas in it."

I pulled out my phone and looked at Bella's food.

Second ingredient: pea protein.

"So the food has taurine, but her body can't use it?"

"Exactly. The peas block absorption. At the shelter, we'd see cats come in eating premium food—$70, $80 bags—and they'd still be severely taurine deficient. That's why we just gave it to everyone. Didn't matter what food they were on."

"And that's why she throws up?"

"Almost certainly. Taurine is critical for digestion. Without enough, the digestive system can't function properly. Cats throw up. They get lethargic. Their coats get dull. All the things you described."

I felt like someone had punched me in the stomach.

"Why didn't my vet tell me this?"

Carol was quiet for a moment.

"Margaret, I don't want to speak badly about your vet. But in 30 years working with shelters, I saw this pattern constantly. Cats would come in from private vets who'd been treating chronic vomiting for months or years. The vet would sell them expensive food, run tests, prescribe medications. But they never tested taurine. And when we did? Almost always deficient."

"So my vet should know about this?"

"Every vet should. But it's not emphasized in private practice the way it is in shelter medicine. At shelters, we can't afford to treat symptoms forever. We have to fix the root cause. That's how I learned about taurine—out of necessity."

I stared at her. "So for two years, Bella's been deficient and my vet never caught it?"

"Probably."

"Where do I get this? Can I just buy it?"

I couldn't believe something this simple existed and I'd never heard of it.

Carol walked to her kitchen and pulled out a white tub from her cabinet.

"This is what I used at the shelter. KittySupps. It's pharmaceutical-grade. After testing dozens of brands over the years, this was the only one that was consistently pure and properly dosed."

She handed me the tub.

"Give Bella one scoop a day mixed into her food. You should see a difference within a week."

I took the tub home.

Started giving it to Bella that same day.

Day 1: She threw up once.

Day 2: No vomiting.

Day 3: No vomiting.

Day 4: No vomiting.

Day 5: Still nothing.

By the end of the first week, I couldn't believe it.

Bella hadn't thrown up once.

Her coat started getting shiny again.

She was playing. Running around.

Acting exactly like she did at Carol's house.

Week two: Still no vomiting.

Week three: Nothing.

By week four, I called Carol.

"It worked. She hasn't thrown up in a month."

"Good," Carol said. "That's what we saw at the shelter every time. Give their bodies the taurine they need, and the vomiting just... stops."

That was eight months ago.

Bella hasn't thrown up once since I started the taurine.

Not once.

And I'm so angry I could cry.

Because this is standard protocol at animal shelters.

Every shelter vet knows about this.

They give taurine to every cat because they've seen this problem thousands of times.

But my private vet? In two years, never mentioned it once.

He just kept selling me expensive food.

Kept running expensive tests.

Kept telling me it was "normal."

And Bella suffered.

I asked Carol why private vets don't do what shelter vets do.

She sighed. "Different incentives, I think. At the shelter, our job was to get cats healthy as quickly and cheaply as possible so they could be adopted. We couldn't afford to manage symptoms forever. We had to fix the root cause."

"And private vets?"

"Private vets make money from ongoing treatment. Prescription food. Monthly vet visits. Tests. Medications. There's no money in telling someone to buy a supplement that fixes the problem permanently."

"That's insane."

"That's the industry. At shelters, we'd joke that if private vets ran shelters the way they run practices, we'd never get a single cat adopted. They'd all be on prescription diets forever."

I started asking around in my Facebook groups.

"Has anyone's vet ever mentioned taurine deficiency?"

Almost no one.

Out of hundreds of responses, maybe five people had vets who tested for it.

Everyone else? Their vets said it was hairballs. Sensitive stomach. "Just what cats do."

And they were all spending hundreds or thousands on food and vet visits.

Just like I had been.

The pet food industry is in on it too.

They know most cats can't absorb the taurine in their food.

They know peas and lentils block absorption.

But they use them anyway because they're cheap.

And they can still claim "taurine added" on the label.

Even though the cat's body can't use it.

It's a scam.

The whole thing is a scam.

And cats like Bella are suffering because of it.

If your cat is vomiting regularly, ask your vet to test their taurine levels.

If your vet says "it's not necessary," find a different vet.

Or better yet, just start giving them taurine supplements.

Because that's what every shelter vet in America does.

It's standard protocol.

And there's a reason for that.

Carol saved Bella's life.

Not by doing anything complicated.

Just by following the same protocol she followed for 30 years at the shelter.

Something my private vet should have known about.

I don't work at a shelter. I'm not a vet.

But I can give Bella what she needs now.

One scoop of KittySupps taurine. Every day. Mixed into her food.

That's it.

Bella hasn't thrown up in eight months.

She's healthy. Happy. Playing.

Just like she was at Carol's house.

If you're reading this and your cat throws up all the time, I'm telling you what your vet won't.

It's probably not hairballs.

It's probably not sensitive stomach.

It's standard protocol at animal shelters to give every cat taurine supplements.

Because they see this problem every single day.

But your private vet either doesn't know or doesn't want you to know.

I'm telling you now because I wish someone had told me two years ago.

The taurine I use is the same one Carol used at the shelter. KittySupps.

I order it online. Takes two days to arrive.

I don't work for them. I don't get paid to say this.

I'm just a regular person who spent two years watching my cat suffer.

And finally found out why.

Here's where I get Bella's taurine: https://kittysupps.com/products/taurine

Your vet won't tell you this is standard protocol at shelters.

The pet food companies won't tell you their food doesn't work.

But I'm telling you.

Because Bella deserves to be healthy.

And so does your cat.

P.S. My neighbor is a retired shelter vet. For 30 years, she gave taurine to every single cat on day one. It's standard protocol at shelters. My private vet never mentioned it once in two years. Ask yourself why.

P.P.S. Animal shelters can't afford to treat symptoms forever. They have to fix the root cause. That's why every shelter cat gets taurine. Your private vet makes more money managing your cat's vomiting than preventing it. One scoop a day fixes it permanently.

My mouth fell open.

"You're a vet?"

"Was. I retired five years ago."

"Why didn't you tell me?"

She shrugged. "You didn't ask. And honestly, I didn't think it mattered. I was just watching your cat."

I leaned forward. "Carol, Bella was perfect at your house for a month. You're a vet. You had to have done something."

"Well," she said. "I did treat Bella the way I treated every cat at the shelter. It's just... automatic for me after 30 years. I don't even think about it anymore."

"What do you mean?"

"At the shelter, we had a protocol. Day one for every cat: check for fleas, make sure they're eating properly, and start them on taurine supplements."

"Taurine? What's that?"

"It's an amino acid. Cats can't make it on their own—they have to get it from food. We saw deficiency in almost every cat that came through our doors. So we just started giving it to everyone automatically."

I stared at her. "You gave Bella taurine?"

"Yes. It's standard protocol. Every single cat at the shelter got it because we saw chronic vomiting, lethargy, dull coats—all signs of deficiency—in probably 80% of the cats we took in."

"But Bella eats expensive food. My vet recommended it. It has everything she needs."

Carol shook her head. "The food might say it has taurine on the label. But most cats can't absorb it properly. Especially if the food has peas or lentils or chickpeas in it."

I pulled out my phone and looked at Bella's food.

Second ingredient: pea protein.

"So the food has taurine, but her body can't use it?"

"Exactly. The peas block absorption. At the shelter, we'd see cats come in eating premium food—$70, $80 bags—and they'd still be severely taurine deficient. That's why we just gave it to everyone. Didn't matter what food they were on."

"And that's why she throws up?"

"Almost certainly. Taurine is critical for digestion. Without enough, the digestive system can't function properly. Cats throw up. They get lethargic. Their coats get dull. All the things you described."

I felt like someone had punched me in the stomach.

"Why didn't my vet tell me this?"

Carol was quiet for a moment.

"Margaret, I don't want to speak badly about your vet. But in 30 years working with shelters, I saw this pattern constantly. Cats would come in from private vets who'd been treating chronic vomiting for months or years. The vet would sell them expensive food, run tests, prescribe medications. But they never tested taurine. And when we did? Almost always deficient."

"So my vet should know about this?"

"Every vet should. But it's not emphasized in private practice the way it is in shelter medicine. At shelters, we can't afford to treat symptoms forever. We have to fix the root cause. That's how I learned about taurine—out of necessity."

I stared at her. "So for two years, Bella's been deficient and my vet never caught it?"

"Probably."

"Where do I get this? Can I just buy it?"

I couldn't believe something this simple existed and I'd never heard of it.

Carol walked to her kitchen and pulled out a white tub from her cabinet.

"This is what I used at the shelter. KittySupps. It's pharmaceutical-grade. After testing dozens of brands over the years, this was the only one that was consistently pure and properly dosed."

She handed me the tub.

"Give Bella one scoop a day mixed into her food. You should see a difference within a week."

I took the tub home.

Started giving it to Bella that same day.

Day 1: She threw up once.

Day 2: No vomiting.

Day 3: No vomiting.

Day 4: No vomiting.

Day 5: Still nothing.

By the end of the first week, I couldn't believe it.

Bella hadn't thrown up once.

Her coat started getting shiny again.

She was playing. Running around.

Acting exactly like she did at Carol's house.

Week two: Still no vomiting.

Week three: Nothing.

By week four, I called Carol.

"It worked. She hasn't thrown up in a month."

"Good," Carol said. "That's what we saw at the shelter every time. Give their bodies the taurine they need, and the vomiting just... stops."

That was eight months ago.

Bella hasn't thrown up once since I started the taurine.

Not once.

And I'm so angry I could cry.

Because this is standard protocol at animal shelters.

Every shelter vet knows about this.

They give taurine to every cat because they've seen this problem thousands of times.

But my private vet? In two years, never mentioned it once.

He just kept selling me expensive food.

Kept running expensive tests.

Kept telling me it was "normal."

And Bella suffered.

I asked Carol why private vets don't do what shelter vets do.

She sighed. "Different incentives, I think. At the shelter, our job was to get cats healthy as quickly and cheaply as possible so they could be adopted. We couldn't afford to manage symptoms forever. We had to fix the root cause."

"And private vets?"

"Private vets make money from ongoing treatment. Prescription food. Monthly vet visits. Tests. Medications. There's no money in telling someone to buy a supplement that fixes the problem permanently."

"That's insane."

"That's the industry. At shelters, we'd joke that if private vets ran shelters the way they run practices, we'd never get a single cat adopted. They'd all be on prescription diets forever."

I started asking around in my Facebook groups.

"Has anyone's vet ever mentioned taurine deficiency?"

Almost no one.

Out of hundreds of responses, maybe five people had vets who tested for it.

Everyone else? Their vets said it was hairballs. Sensitive stomach. "Just what cats do."

And they were all spending hundreds or thousands on food and vet visits.

Just like I had been.

The pet food industry is in on it too.

They know most cats can't absorb the taurine in their food.

They know peas and lentils block absorption.

But they use them anyway because they're cheap.

And they can still claim "taurine added" on the label.

Even though the cat's body can't use it.

It's a scam.

The whole thing is a scam.

And cats like Bella are suffering because of it.

If your cat is vomiting regularly, ask your vet to test their taurine levels.

If your vet says "it's not necessary," find a different vet.

Or better yet, just start giving them taurine supplements.

Because that's what every shelter vet in America does.

It's standard protocol.

And there's a reason for that.

Carol saved Bella's life.

Not by doing anything complicated.

Just by following the same protocol she followed for 30 years at the shelter.

Something my private vet should have known about.

I don't work at a shelter. I'm not a vet.

But I can give Bella what she needs now.

One scoop of KittySupps taurine. Every day. Mixed into her food.

That's it.

Bella hasn't thrown up in eight months.

She's healthy. Happy. Playing.

Just like she was at Carol's house.

If you're reading this and your cat throws up all the time, I'm telling you what your vet won't.

It's probably not hairballs.

It's probably not sensitive stomach.

It's standard protocol at animal shelters to give every cat taurine supplements.

Because they see this problem every single day.

But your private vet either doesn't know or doesn't want you to know.

I'm telling you now because I wish someone had told me two years ago.

The taurine I use is the same one Carol used at the shelter. KittySupps.

I order it online. Takes two days to arrive.

I don't work for them. I don't get paid to say this.

I'm just a regular person who spent two years watching my cat suffer.

And finally found out why.

Here's where I get Bella's taurine: https://kittysupps.com/products/taurine

Your vet won't tell you this is standard protocol at shelters.

The pet food companies won't tell you their food doesn't work.

But I'm telling you.

Because Bella deserves to be healthy.

And so does your cat.

P.S. My neighbor is a retired shelter vet. For 30 years, she gave taurine to every single cat on day one. It's standard protocol at shelters. My private vet never mentioned it once in two years. Ask yourself why.

P.P.S. Animal shelters can't afford to treat symptoms forever. They have to fix the root cause. That's why every shelter cat gets taurine. Your private vet makes more money managing your cat's vomiting than preventing it. One scoop a day fixes it permanently.

headline: PSA For black people ONLY
body:
If you're Black and diabetic, there's a biological reason your A1C won't budge below 7.0... and it's NOT your diet…

I need you to hear this.

Because what I'm about to share with you could be the difference between staying diabetic for life... and achieving remission within the next 3 to 6 months.

My name is Dr. Quintavius Carter, and I've spent 22 years as an Endocrinologist specializing in diabetes and metabolic disorders.

And over those 22 years, I've watched thousands of Black folks come through the ER with blood sugar over 300... with many of them already losing their vision, losing feeling in their feet and already with fighting wounds that REFUSE to heal.

And almost every single one of them says the same thing:

"Doc, I don't understand. I've been doing everything right. I cut out the sugar. I've been walking every day. I'm taking my metformin. But my A1C just won't budge."

But the problem is, there's a specific biological reason why your A1C won't budge below 7.0 no matter how clean you eat or how much metformin you take.

In fact, it's the same reason you developed diabetes in the first place…

Which, contrary to popular belief, has nothing to do with your diet, your weight, or how much you exercise.

It has to do with the melanin in our skin.

Let me explain.

You see, our melanin is beautiful. It makes us look like kings and queens and it protects us from sun burns.
But according to research published by the University of Houston…our melanin blocks about 90% of the UV rays we need to produce Vitamin D naturally…

So because of our melanin…a Black person needs 3 to 5 times more sun exposure than a white person to make the same amount of Vitamin D.

But we aren't getting it. And it is killing us.

For context, the optimal Vitamin D levels are 50 ng/mL…

But the average Black man and woman with diabetes has Vitamin D levels of 11 ng/mL…

Which is DANGEROUSLY low…

And when you have low Vitamin D like this, THREE specific things happen that will destroy your body from the inside out and ruin your chances of remission…

👉 The first thing is your cells stop responding to insulin.

You see, according to a study published in the Journal of Molecular Biochemistry, Vitamin D is what activates a protein called IRS-1 (Insulin Receptor Substrate 1).

This protein is like the "signal relay" inside your cells that tells glucose transporters to come to the surface and let glucose inside.

But when you're deficient in Vitamin D, IRS-1 stops working.

Even when insulin binds to your cell's receptor, the signal never gets relayed through.

Your cells cannot absorb sugar, and glucose begins piling up in your bloodstream.

It's like having a doorbell that's broken. The delivery driver (insulin) keeps ringing, but nobody inside the house can hear it, so the packages (glucose) just sit outside, piling up on the porch.

This is why your blood sugar won't come down no matter how much metformin you take.

Because metformin can't fix IRS-1. Only Vitamin D can.

But there's an even more dangerous side to it. 

👉 And it has to do with your pancreas.

You see, research published in the Journal of Clinical Investigation has shown that when your pancreas sees all this sugar building up in your blood, it goes into panic mode.

The beta cells in your pancreas start overproducing insulin at five to ten times the normal 
rate, desperately trying to overcome the cellular resistance.

During this phase, your blood sugar readings might look somewhat controlled on a standard 
test.

But your system is under extreme metabolic stress.

Your pancreas is working overtime, 24/7, trying to force glucose into resistant cells.

And here's where it gets deadly.

Every time your pancreas secretes insulin, it also secretes a protein called Amylin.

Now when you have optimal Vitamin D levels, this is managed effectively and causes no problems.

But when you're Vitamin D deficient, your body can't clear the Amylin fast enough.

And when your pancreas is overproducing insulin at 5-10x the normal rate, it's also overproducing Amylin at 5-10x the normal rate.

This excess Amylin…with little to no Vitamin D to break it down…begins to aggregate into toxic amyloid plaques directly within your pancreatic tissue.

These plaques are physically destructive.

They surround and suffocate the healthy beta cells, cutting off their blood supply and preventing them from functioning.

👉 But here's the third thing…and this is what makes remission near IMPOSSIBLE with low Vitamin D levels…

The actual release of insulin from your pancreas is dependent on voltage-gated calcium channels.

Vitamin D is the primary regulator of these channels.

When Vitamin D is low, your pancreas cannot manage the flow of calcium needed to trigger the release of insulin.

Even if your pancreas has insulin ready to go, it lacks Vitamin D, which is essentially the chemical "spark" required to push that insulin into your bloodstream.

It's like having a loaded gun with no firing pin. All the ammunition is there, but there's no way to fire it.

This leads to a total breakdown in blood sugar regulation.

And once that breakdown happens, your beta cells start dying.

The combination of amyloid plaque buildup and the sheer exhaustion of overproducing 
insulin causes your beta cells to die off.

This is called Apoptosis…programmed cell death.

It's your body's way of eliminating cells that are too damaged to function anymore.

And when your beta cells die, they don't come back.

You can lose up to 50% of your insulin-producing capacity in a very short window of time.

At this point, your pancreas can no longer keep up with the rising glucose.

Your fasting blood sugar levels spike into the severely diabetic range: 180 mg/dL… 200 mg/dL… sometimes over 300 mg/dL.

This is what's keeping you from remission.

👉 Your IRS-1 protein is shut down. Your pancreas is suffocating from amyloid plaques. Your 
calcium channels aren't working.

And it’s all because YOU are Vitamin D deficient.

This deficiency is why Type 2 diabetes affects us black folks at TWICE the rate it affects white folks.

It’s also why our A1C struggles to drop below 7.0 no matter what we do.

Because we're walking around with a Vitamin D deficiency that's destroying our ability to 
control blood sugar at the cellular level.

And the worst part is…

Almost no doctor talks about this. They don't even teach it in medical school.

Mainly because Vitamin D deficiency and diabetes is something that disproportionately 
affects Black folks... and the American medical system doesn't care about us.

That's why we still have Black people dealing with things like sickle cell... hypertension...

It's also why so many Black women die during childbirth.

The system just isn't set up to cater to us.

The only reason I know about this is because I saw way too many Black men and women in 
their 40s and 50s get brought in with blood sugar over 300... already losing their vision, 
already with wounds that won't heal... and many of them didn't make it.

But I'd rarely ever see white folks being rushed in like that for diabetes complications at that age.

So I started to do my own research... combing through scientific journals... listening to Dr. Sebi's old seminars.

And that's when I discovered the link between Vitamin D deficiency and Type 2 diabetes in Black people.

Now listen carefully, because what I'm about to tell you could save your life.

You might be thinking, "Okay, I'll just go grab a Vitamin D pill from CVS on my way home."

DO NOT DO THAT.

🛑Taking Vitamin D alone won't get you into remission…and could actually cause other 
Problems.

👉Here's why.

Flooding your body with Vitamin D supplements will help restore your IRS-1 protein and reactivate your insulin signaling.

But without something to direct calcium properly, you're going to run into another deadly problem.

You see, when you take Vitamin D, it helps your body absorb more calcium from food.

Which sounds great, right?

But here's the catch.

That calcium doesn't automatically know where to go.

It just floats around aimlessly in your bloodstream, drifting through your arteries and soft tissues, looking for a home.

And when you're diabetic, your arteries are already damaged from years of high blood sugar.

That damage has turned the smooth inner walls rough and jagged... like sandpaper instead of glass.

In healthy arteries, calcium just flows right past the smooth walls.

But in damaged arteries…which you have if you're diabetic…that floating calcium gets stuck on the rough spots where it settles and builds up layer by layer.

And once enough of it builds up, it turns into plaque... and blocks the flow of blood and oxygen to your heart.

Diabetics are ALREADY at extremely high risk of heart attacks and strokes because of arterial calcification.

Taking Vitamin D alone makes it worse, not better.

You need something to tell the calcium exactly where to go.

👉 And that thing is called Vitamin K2.

Vitamin K2 acts like a GPS system for calcium. It directs it away from your arteries and towards your bones and teeth, where the calcium belongs.

So if you're going to take a Vitamin D3 supplement, you also NEED to take K2.

But here's the thing…

👉Even if you have the Vitamin D (to restore IRS-1) and the K2 (to direct calcium)... you still also NEED Magnesium.

That's because Vitamin D is actually a hormone, not just a vitamin.

And when you swallow it, it enters your body in a "dormant" state.

But Magnesium is the "Ignition Key" that gets it going.

Think of it like a car with a full tank of gas. If you don't turn the key or push the button to start it, that car isn't going anywhere.

Magnesium is what physically converts Vitamin D from its inactive form into its active form.

Without magnesium, that Vitamin D just sits in your liver, doing absolutely nothing.

And here's the other critical thing about magnesium for diabetics:

Magnesium ALSO directly improves insulin sensitivity on its own.

It helps your cells respond to insulin signals independent of Vitamin D.

Studies show that 48% of diabetics are ALSO magnesium deficient, which makes their insulin resistance even worse.

So magnesium is doing double duty: activating your Vitamin D AND helping your cells respond to insulin.

👉And beyond Magnesium... you also need MCT oil.

I know what you're thinking. "ANOTHER INGREDIENT???"

But this is EXTREMELY important. Let me explain why.

Blood is water-based, but Vitamin D is fat-based.

They don't mix.

So if you just take Vitamin D3 with water, your body will struggle to absorb it.

Most of it will just slide right through your digestive system and end up in the toilet.

You're absorbing maybe 10-20% at best.

So you need a carrier, and that carrier is MCT Oil.

MCT Oil acts like a delivery truck.

It surrounds the Vitamin D, protects it, and drives it through the gut wall directly into the bloodstream.

With MCT oil, you're absorbing 80-90% of the Vitamin D.

That's a massive difference when you're trying to restore IRS-1 function and get your blood sugar under control.

So let me count this off for you:

✅ MCT Oil delivers the Vitamin D into your blood.

✅ Magnesium activates the Vitamin D and improves insulin sensitivity.

✅ Vitamin D restores your IRS-1 protein so your cells can accept glucose.

✅ Vitamin K2 directs calcium away from your arteries and into your bones.

You need all four steps. If you miss one, the whole chain breaks.

Now, I know what you're thinking. "Now I need to go out and buy four different supplements? hell naw"

Well, I have good news.

There's a company that puts all those ingredients into one formula that we Black folks with diabetes can take to ramp up our Vitamin D levels and finally achieve remission.

I personally take it and give it to my parents who are pre-diabetic.

It's a completely natural blend of Vitamin D3, Vitamin K2, Magnesium Glycinate, and Organic MCT Oil.

And it's called "True Nutra's D3 + K2."

It's the only one I've found that combines a high therapeutic dose of Vitamin D3, the right form of K2 (MK-7), Magnesium Glycinate, and MCT Coconut Oil into one formula.

I have to warn you... it is not sold in stores. You have to order it online.

But this stuff is a godsend for Black folks with diabetes... better than any additional pharma pill I could prescribe right now.

I've recommended True Nutra's D3 + K2 to dozens of my diabetic patients who were struggling to get their A1C below 7.0.

And every single one of them thanks me for it.

In fact, here's what my patients typically experience within the first 12 weeks of taking True Nutra's D3 + K2:

✅ Week 1 - 3: The post-meal crashes disappear. No more falling asleep after lunch. Their energy stabilizes throughout the day instead of spiking and crashing.
✅ Week 4 - 6: When they check their fasting blood sugar in the morning, it starts trending down. 155 mg/dL drops to 140... then 125... then 110. They're shocked at how fast it improves.
✅ Week 7 - 9: They come back for follow-ups and I run the labs. Their A1C has dropped 0.5-0.8 points. Their Vitamin D levels are up to 50-65 ng/mL. Their fasting insulin has normalized.
✅ Week 10 - 12: Many of my patients have achieved remission…A1C below 6.5% without medication. I've been able to reduce or eliminate their metformin under my supervision. They can't believe they're finally in remission after years of struggling.

Now, before I made this post, I reached out to the good folks over at True Nutra to tell them I was going to expose what I'm seeing happening to Black folks with diabetes.

They were so moved that they wanted to help get this supplement into the hands of more brothers and sisters.

So right now, they've set up a special link where you can get a supply of True Nutra's D3 + K2 for just $27.95.

That is a massive 69% discount.

BUT…it gets even better.

You see, True Nutra knows that usually, when one person has diabetes... they might also have a wife, a husband, or just people they care about who are also diabetic or pre-diabetic.

Diabetes runs in families…not just because of genetics, but because we all share the same melanin blocking our Vitamin D production.

So for a limited time, they are doing a Buy One Get One FREE offer at the same price of $27.95.

That means you can get a bottle for yourself and a bottle for your spouse or family member for next to nothing.

They are practically giving it away because they know once you see your A1C drop into remission range... you will want to be a customer for life.

So if you'd like to give it a try... click here to check if it's still available:

👉 https://truenutrawellness.com/products/fb-en-vdk002

I know they sell out pretty quickly, so do yourself a favor and take advantage of the 69% discount + buy one get one free offer before they sell out.

And the best part about all this is they offer a 30-day money-back guarantee.

Which means you can try True Nutra's D3 + K2 formula for 30 days... see if your fasting blood sugar drops, see if your energy stabilizes, see if your A1C starts trending down... and if you don't feel a difference, they will refund every penny.

So don't wait until you're losing your vision or losing feeling in your feet.

Don't wait until your kidneys start failing or you develop heart disease.

We have to take care of our own health because the system isn't going to do it for us.

Click the link below to check if True Nutra still has the D3 + K2 supplement in stock:

👉 https://truenutrawellness.com/products/fb-en-vdk002

If they're in stock, grab a bundle.

You'll be glad you did.

Diabetics are ALREADY at extremely high risk of heart attacks and strokes because of arterial calcification.

Taking Vitamin D alone makes it worse, not better.

You need something to tell the calcium exactly where to go.

👉 And that thing is called Vitamin K2.

Vitamin K2 acts like a GPS system for calcium. It directs it away from your arteries and towards your bones and teeth, where the calcium belongs.

So if you're going to take a Vitamin D3 supplement, you also NEED to take K2.

But here's the thing…

👉Even if you have the Vitamin D (to restore IRS-1) and the K2 (to direct calcium)... you still also NEED Magnesium.

That's because Vitamin D is actually a hormone, not just a vitamin.

And when you swallow it, it enters your body in a "dormant" state.

But Magnesium is the "Ignition Key" that gets it going.

Think of it like a car with a full tank of gas. If you don't turn the key or push the button to start it, that car isn't going anywhere.

Magnesium is what physically converts Vitamin D from its inactive form into its active form.

Without magnesium, that Vitamin D just sits in your liver, doing absolutely nothing.

And here's the other critical thing about magnesium for diabetics:

Magnesium ALSO directly improves insulin sensitivity on its own.

It helps your cells respond to insulin signals independent of Vitamin D.

Studies show that 48% of diabetics are ALSO magnesium deficient, which makes their insulin resistance even worse.

So magnesium is doing double duty: activating your Vitamin D AND helping your cells respond to insulin.

👉And beyond Magnesium... you also need MCT oil.

I know what you're thinking. "ANOTHER INGREDIENT???"

But this is EXTREMELY important. Let me explain why.

Blood is water-based, but Vitamin D is fat-based.

They don't mix.

So if you just take Vitamin D3 with water, your body will struggle to absorb it.

Most of it will just slide right through your digestive system and end up in the toilet.

You're absorbing maybe 10-20% at best.

So you need a carrier, and that carrier is MCT Oil.

MCT Oil acts like a delivery truck.

It surrounds the Vitamin D, protects it, and drives it through the gut wall directly into the bloodstream.

With MCT oil, you're absorbing 80-90% of the Vitamin D.

That's a massive difference when you're trying to restore IRS-1 function and get your blood sugar under control.

So let me count this off for you:

✅ MCT Oil delivers the Vitamin D into your blood.

✅ Magnesium activates the Vitamin D and improves insulin sensitivity.

✅ Vitamin D restores your IRS-1 protein so your cells can accept glucose.

✅ Vitamin K2 directs calcium away from your arteries and into your bones.

You need all four steps. If you miss one, the whole chain breaks.

Now, I know what you're thinking. "Now I need to go out and buy four different supplements? hell naw"

Well, I have good news.

There's a company that puts all those ingredients into one formula that we Black folks with diabetes can take to ramp up our Vitamin D levels and finally achieve remission.

I personally take it and give it to my parents who are pre-diabetic.

It's a completely natural blend of Vitamin D3, Vitamin K2, Magnesium Glycinate, and Organic MCT Oil.

And it's called "True Nutra's D3 + K2."

It's the only one I've found that combines a high therapeutic dose of Vitamin D3, the right form of K2 (MK-7), Magnesium Glycinate, and MCT Coconut Oil into one formula.

I have to warn you... it is not sold in stores. You have to order it online.

But this stuff is a godsend for Black folks with diabetes... better than any additional pharma pill I could prescribe right now.

I've recommended True Nutra's D3 + K2 to dozens of my diabetic patients who were struggling to get their A1C below 7.0.

And every single one of them thanks me for it.

In fact, here's what my patients typically experience within the first 12 weeks of taking True Nutra's D3 + K2:

✅ Week 1 - 3: The post-meal crashes disappear. No more falling asleep after lunch. Their energy stabilizes throughout the day instead of spiking and crashing.

✅ Week 7 - 9: They come back for follow-ups and I run the labs. Their A1C has dropped 0.5-0.8 points. Their Vitamin D levels are up to 50-65 ng/mL. Their fasting insulin has normalized.

Now, before I made this post, I reached out to the good folks over at True Nutra to tell them I was going to expose what I'm seeing happening to Black folks with diabetes.
