headline: Feel Like Yourself Again
body:
I was sitting in a Cracker Barrel parking lot in Bowling Green, Kentucky at 7:14 in the morning when my phone rang and my sister told me she was alive but she didn't know how.

That's the sentence I keep coming back to.

She didn't say I'm in the hospital. She didn't say I had a heart attack. She said I'm alive but I don't know how, and the way she said it, with this thin reedy quality I'd never heard come out of her before, is the reason I'm writing this 11 months later instead of going about my Tuesday.

My name is Lorraine. I'm 61 years old. I live in Elizabethtown, Kentucky, which is about 45 minutes south of Louisville and not somewhere most people have ever heard of unless they had a relative stationed at Fort Knox. I taught third grade for 32 years at the same elementary school. I retired in May. My husband Phil drives a route truck for a uniform company and is gone 4 days a week. We have 1 daughter who lives in Lexington with her husband and our 2 grandbabies.

My sister Brenda is 4 years younger than me. She lives outside Indianapolis with her husband Dean. She works as an office manager for a dental practice. She's the kind of person who keeps the family birthdays in a paper book even though she has a phone. She's the one who organizes Thanksgiving and remembers what kind of pie our father liked and calls me on Sunday afternoons just to talk about nothing.

She was 57 the morning she called me from that hospital bed.

And I want to be clear about something before I go any further. Brenda is not a person who has ever taken her health for granted. She walks. She doesn't smoke. She gave up sweet tea in 2013. She does the things you're supposed to do.

What she had was high blood pressure. That's all. She had been managing it with a pill her doctor prescribed when she was 51. That was the whole story.

She had been to the ER once before, the year prior, for what they called a "spell." Lightheaded. Pressure was through the roof when they took it. They adjusted her medication, sent her home, told her to monitor it. She did monitor it. She bought one of those little arm cuffs from CVS and she'd take her reading every morning before her coffee and write it down in a spiral notebook.

The numbers were never quite where they should be. She'd tell me sometimes on those Sunday calls. She'd say, "It was 158 over 94 this morning, Reenie." She had been calling me Reenie since she could talk.

I'd say, "Are you taking your pill?"

She'd say, "Of course I'm taking my pill."

And we'd talk about the grandbabies.

The morning she called me from the hospital was a Tuesday in March. I had gotten in the car early to meet a friend named Patty for breakfast on her way through town. We were going to eat at the Cracker Barrel off the interstate because that's where you eat breakfast when you're 61 and your friend is driving from Nashville to Cincinnati to see her son.

I was sitting in the parking lot with my engine off, watching the sun come up over the gas station next door, waiting for Patty's truck to pull in.

My phone rang.

The caller was Brenda's husband Dean, which was wrong, because Dean does not call me. Dean is one of those men who lets his wife handle all the relationships. If Dean was calling me at 7:14 on a Tuesday morning, something had happened.

I answered and Dean said, "She's okay, Lorraine. She's okay, but I'm putting her on."

Then Brenda was on the phone, and her voice did that thin reedy thing, and she said the sentence.

She said she had been getting ready for work. She said she felt the room tip. She said the next thing she remembered was being on the bathroom floor with Dean kneeling over her, and Dean said she had been out for almost a minute. They drove to the ER. The pressure reading they took when she walked in was 198 over 116.

A nurse who took the reading said the words "you got lucky" out loud.

Brenda said it through the phone twice. "I got lucky, Reenie. I got lucky."

I sat in that Cracker Barrel parking lot and I watched the sun come over the gas station and I felt something shift in my body that I had been ignoring for probably 4 years.

Because here's the thing nobody knew until I sat in that parking lot.

I was Brenda.

I was Brenda in slow motion. I had been Brenda for years.

My pressure had been creeping up since I was 55. My doctor had me on a low dose of something called amlodipine and told me to watch my salt and gave me a pamphlet. The same kind of pamphlet they gave Brenda. I had been taking the pill. I had been watching my salt. My morning numbers were not where they were supposed to be either, and I had been doing what people do when they're afraid, which is just not looking at the numbers very hard.

I had a cuff from CVS too. Same brand as Brenda's. I'd take a reading and if it was bad I'd take another reading 5 minutes later and tell myself the second one was the real one.

I had been telling myself things.

I had been telling myself that the headaches behind my eyes in the afternoons were because I was looking at screens too much. I had been telling myself the buzzing in my ears at night was just what 61 sounded like. I had been telling myself my heart pounding when I bent over to tie my shoes was normal because I was, and I want to use the polite word here, a soft 61 year old woman who needed to lose some weight.

None of those things are normal. I knew none of those things were normal.

I just didn't want to be Brenda.

And then Brenda was on the phone telling me she had been on the bathroom floor.

I drove home that morning instead of having breakfast with Patty. I texted Patty in the parking lot and told her family emergency. I drove the 16 miles back to my house and I walked in the front door and I sat at my kitchen table and I did not move for almost an hour.

Then I got up and I called my doctor's office and I asked them what else there was.

The receptionist put me through to a nurse practitioner named Sheila who I had been seeing for years. Sheila and I had a long history. She had walked me through perimenopause and she had walked me through my mother's diagnosis and she was the kind of medical person who actually talked to you like a human being.

I told her what had happened to Brenda and I told her my numbers had been bad too and I told her I needed to know what else there was beyond what I was already doing.

Sheila said something I didn't expect.

She said, "Lorraine, the medication you're on, it's a tool. It's not the whole story. The reason your numbers haven't moved is because we're treating the pressure but we haven't been talking about the wall."

I said, what wall.

She said, "The wall of your blood vessels. The inside lining."

And then she explained something to me at the kitchen table over the phone for about 20 minutes that I had never had explained to me by any doctor in my entire life.

The inside of your blood vessels has a living lining called the endothelium. It's a single layer of cells that runs through every vessel in your body. That lining is responsible for producing a molecule called nitric oxide that tells your vessels when to relax. When the lining is healthy, it's producing the nitric oxide your vessels need, and your vessels stay flexible and open the way they're supposed to.

The problem, Sheila said, is that the lining takes damage over the years. From inflammation. From oxidative stress. From processed food. From all the same garbage that ages every other part of you. And when that lining is worn down, it can't produce nitric oxide the way it used to. So your vessels stay tight. Blood has to push harder to get through. The pressure goes up.

The blood pressure pill, she said, forces the vessels to relax through a different mechanism. It bypasses the lining. It makes the vessels open up using a different door. Which is fine. It works. It moves your numbers.

But it doesn't do anything to support the lining itself.

Which means underneath the medication, the wall is still wearing down. The cycle is still feeding itself. And that's why so many people on blood pressure medication have to keep adjusting their dose upward as the years go on. Not because the medication stops working. Because the wall keeps deteriorating.

She said this very plainly and not in a sales way and in fact she said she wasn't recommending I stop my medication, just that I should be thinking about the wall too.

I said, what supports the wall.

And she said, well, that's where it gets interesting.

She told me about a fruit.

I'm going to admit something. When she said the words "Indian gooseberry" on the phone that morning, I almost laughed. Not because I thought she was lying. Because I had spent 4 years on a prescription medication for high blood pressure and the most basic conversation about the actual mechanism of my disease was happening with a nurse practitioner because of a phone call from a hospital bed in Indianapolis.

I almost laughed and then I almost cried.

Sheila said the fruit was called amla. She said there were compounds in it called emblicanins that had been studied for their effect on the vascular lining. She said the research suggested those compounds appeared to support the endothelial lining's ability to produce nitric oxide. Which meant, in plain English, that they could potentially help the wall do its job again, instead of bypassing the wall like the medication did.

She said she had several patients on it.

She said she'd been taking it herself for about 8 months.

She said her own pressure had come down 14 points and her energy had come back and her doctor at her own physical had asked what she was doing differently.

I asked her what she took specifically.

She told me she took Alevia Amla, that she'd tried 2 other brands first and not noticed much, and that this one had been different for her.

I wrote the name down on the back of a grocery receipt that was sitting on my kitchen table. I still have that receipt. I keep it in the drawer with my batteries because I don't know where else to put it.

I'd love to tell you I ordered it that day. I didn't.

I sat with the information for about 6 days. I went and saw Brenda the following weekend. I drove up to Indianapolis on Friday afternoon and I sat with her in her living room and I held her hand and we cried a little and then we did what sisters do, which is talk about everything except the thing we'd just cried about.

She told me a lot of things that weekend.

She told me she had been afraid to call me sometimes because she didn't want to worry me. She told me she had been having headaches like mine. She told me she had also been ignoring the buzzing in her ears. She told me she had a moment, the second before she went down on the bathroom floor, where she thought, oh, this is what it feels like.

She said it just like that. She said, "I thought, oh, this is what it feels like."

I drove home Sunday night down 65 with that sentence in my head the entire way and I pulled into my driveway around 11 and I sat in my car and I ordered Alevia Amla on my phone in the dark before I even went inside.

It came on Wednesday.

I started taking 2 capsules every morning with my coffee.

Phil noticed the bottle on the counter and asked what it was. I told him. He said, "Whatever you say, Reenie." Phil is not a curious man about supplements. Phil is curious about lawnmower engines and the Cincinnati Reds and that's about it. He let me do my thing.

The first 10 days, my numbers didn't really move. I want to be honest about that. My morning reading was still high. I want to say this because I think the dishonest version of these stories is when somebody says they tried something for 3 days and everything was perfect. That's not what happened.

What happened in the first 10 days was something smaller.

The buzzing in my ears at night got quieter. Not gone. Quieter.

I noticed it the third night. I was lying in bed waiting to fall asleep and I realized I'd been listening for it and it wasn't there the way it usually was. I rolled over and went to sleep without thinking too hard about it.

By day 14, the buzzing was gone. Just gone. I noticed because I noticed its absence.

By day 21, my morning reading was 142 over 88. Which is still not where it's supposed to be, but it was the lowest morning reading I had taken in over 2 years. I took it twice to be sure. Both readings were the same.

By day 30, the headaches behind my eyes had stopped.

By day 45, my morning reading was 134 over 84.

By day 60, I was walking faster on my morning loop around the cul-de-sac without my heart pounding when I got back. I had been adding distance without realizing it. Phil came home that Friday and asked if I'd lost weight and I said no, and he said, "Well something's different."

By day 75, I called Brenda and I told her everything I'd been doing. I told her about Sheila. I told her about the wall. I told her about the lining and the nitric oxide and the emblicanins and the bottle on my counter. I told her if she wanted to try it, I'd send her one.

She said send it. She had been doing some reading too. Her doctor in Indianapolis hadn't said anything about a wall. She had been on her phone late at night looking up things she didn't understand.

I sent it that afternoon.

By day 90, when I went in for my follow up with Sheila, my pressure in the office was 128 over 80. Sheila looked at the screen and looked at me and she didn't say anything for a second. Then she said, "Tell me what you've been doing."

I told her.

She wrote in my chart and we talked about the next 90 days.

I drove home crying with my hands on the wheel and the radio off.

I want to address some things directly because I know what you're thinking when you read something like this.

You're thinking, this lady doesn't know if it was the amla or the placebo effect or just paying more attention to her health.

I'd say, you might be right. I'm not a doctor. I'm a retired third grade teacher. I cannot prove to you in a rigorous way that the capsules in the bottle on my counter are the reason my buzzing stopped or the reason my morning numbers dropped 24 points. What I can tell you is that I am 61 years old and I have lived in this body for the entire time and I know the difference between what I usually feel like and what I have felt like for the last 9 months, and the difference is not subtle and it is not in my head.

You're also thinking, if this works the way I'm describing, your doctor would have told you about it.

I'd say, my doctor didn't tell me. A nurse practitioner who I have known for 17 years told me. And she only told me because I called her in a moment of fear after my sister almost died and I said I needed to know what else there was. None of the doctors I have seen in my life ever opened a conversation about the wall of my blood vessels. They opened conversations about a number on a chart and a pill that moves the number. That's what they're trained to do and I don't think most of them are bad people. I just think the conversation has been narrow for a very long time, and the people having broader conversations are not always the ones with MD after their name.

You're thinking, a berry. How could a berry do anything.

I'd say, I had the same thought. The first time Sheila said the words Indian gooseberry I almost laughed. Then I read the actual research, and the actual research is not about the berry. It's about specific compounds that have been studied in real clinical trials with real protocols. The berry is the delivery vehicle. The compounds are the thing.

You're thinking, why hasn't anybody else heard about this.

I'd say, people are starting to. Sheila told me she has 30 something patients on it now. The reason it doesn't get talked about loudly is because it doesn't have a pharmaceutical company behind it and there's no advertising budget. It just spreads quietly through people like me telling people like you what happened.

Brenda has been on Alevia Amla for 7 months now.

Her last reading was 138 over 86 and her doctor in Indianapolis has reduced her medication by half, on his recommendation, and is talking about reducing it further. She walks 2 miles every morning. She told me last Sunday she feels like she did in her 40s.

We talk every Sunday. She still calls me Reenie.

I think about that morning in the Cracker Barrel parking lot a lot. I think about what it would have been if Dean hadn't been home. I think about what would have been if my sister had gone down on the bathroom floor by herself and stayed there. I think about how the wall of her blood vessels was wearing down for years and nobody had ever said the word wall to her.

I think about how close it got.

If you have been quietly watching your own numbers creep up year after year, or you've been watching it happen to someone you love, and the only conversation you've ever had about it is the one about the medication and the salt and the pamphlet, I would say what Sheila said to me. The pill is a tool. It is not the whole story. There is a wall, and the wall takes care can be supported, and the body is not as broken as it looks from the outside.

Alevia Amla is what I take. It comes in a 30 day bottle. The company offers a 90 day money back guarantee, which is the same window of time that mattered for me. If you try it for 90 days and nothing changes, you call them and they refund you and you're out nothing except a phone call.

That's the part that mattered to me when I was deciding. There was no risk. There was just a chance.

I'm 61 years old. I took the chance.

I haven't had a buzzing in my ears in 9 months.

👉 https://alevia.com/amla/33

P.S. Brenda asked me to mention that her doctor in Indianapolis, the one reducing her medication, did not know what she was taking when he reduced it. He just looked at her numbers. She told him after. He didn't say much. She thinks he was a little annoyed. She doesn't care.

P.P.S. The 90 day guarantee is what made me try it. I want to say that plainly. I am a careful person with my money. I do not buy things off the internet on faith. The fact that I could try it for 3 months and send it back if nothing happened is the reason I clicked the button in my driveway that Sunday night. If the company didn't stand behind it, I wouldn't have. They do, so I did.

P.P.P.S. Sheila is still my nurse practitioner. She still takes the amla every morning. She has 32 patients on it now, by her last count. None of them are on Facebook writing about it. I'm the first one she knows of. She told me to be honest and not to oversell anything, which is why I have tried not to oversell anything in this post. The capsules are not magic. They are something that, in my body and in my sister's body and in 30 something other bodies in Sheila's practice, appears to be doing something the medication couldn't.

That's all.

I hope this helps somebody.

👉 alevia.com/amla/33

headline: Feel Like Yourself Again
body:
My husband sat on the bottom rung of his own ladder last Tuesday and couldn't make himself climb it.

He's been a roofer in Macon for 38 years.

He built that ladder a reputation. Not the other way around.

I pulled into the driveway around 7:15 in the evening and there he was, still in his harness, sitting on the bottom rung with his hard hat in his lap, watching the sun go down behind the Hendersons' magnolia tree across the street.

He didn't see me pull in.

I sat in the car for a full minute with the engine off, just watching him.

A man I've been married to for 34 years. A man who used to scramble up a 32-foot extension ladder like it was a step stool. Sitting on the first rung like a child who couldn't reach the kitchen counter.

I didn't get out right away.

Because I knew if I got out, he'd stand up. He'd say he was just resting his eyes. He'd haul that ladder back into the bed of the truck like nothing happened, and we'd eat dinner, and he'd pretend, and I'd pretend, and we'd keep pretending.

So I sat there in the car and I cried, and I'm telling you about it now because if you've ever watched a strong man sit down and not get back up, you already know exactly what I'm talking about, and the rest of this might be the most important thing you read this month.

Let me back up.

My name is Janelle. I'm 57. I work part time at the front desk of a dental office on Pio Nono Avenue and I've lived in the same house in Macon, Georgia my entire adult life.

My husband's name is Dale.

Dale Whitfield. Whitfield Roofing & Gutters. The name is on the side of the truck. The name is on the polo shirts. The name is on the magnetic sign we stick to my Buick when I help him drive supplies out to a job in Warner Robins.

Dale started roofing when he was 18 years old. He worked for a man named Henry Pelt for 12 years, learned everything he knew, and started his own company in 1998 with a used Ford F-150 and 1,200 dollars he'd saved up in a coffee can.

He's never missed a day of work in his life. Not when his back went out in 2007. Not when his daddy passed in 2014. Not when our daughter Lacey had her appendix out and he climbed off a roof in Forsyth and drove 90 miles back to the hospital and was back on the same roof by sunrise the next day.

That's the man I'm telling you about.

That's the man who couldn't make himself climb his own ladder.

It started about 18 months ago. I want to say it started before that, but 18 months is when I first noticed something I couldn't explain away.

He came home from a job in Gray and sat down in his recliner without taking off his boots.

Dale never sits in the chair with his boots on. Ever. He stands at the back door and unlaces them and sets them on the rubber mat and then he comes in. That's been the rule for 34 years.

But that night he just walked past the back door, past the mat, past the rule, and sank into the chair and closed his eyes.

I asked him if he was okay.

He said, "I'm just a little wiped, sweetheart."

A little wiped.

That was the start of the lie. Not a big lie. A little one. The kind of lie a man tells himself first and his wife second.

Three weeks later he came home and his face was the color of the inside of a dirty dishwasher. Grey. Slack. Wet around the temples even though it had been overcast all day.

I asked him what happened.

He said the heat got to him on the Henderson tear-off.

It was March. It was 64 degrees.

I went to the medicine cabinet that night and I looked at the bottle of lisinopril he'd been taking since 2018. Twenty milligrams. Once a day. His doctor had told him at his last physical that his pressure was creeping up again and they were probably going to need to add another pill to the regimen.

I stood there at the bathroom sink holding that orange bottle and I thought about all the men I'd seen go this way in our neighborhood.

Bill Thurman across the street. Roofer. Sat down in his recliner one Saturday afternoon at 61 and never really got back up. Just slowly faded. Stopped going to the shop. Stopped going to church. Then one morning he didn't wake up.

Earl Conyers two doors down. Carpenter. Same story. Different chair.

My own father, who drove a milk truck for Mayfield Dairy until he was 66 and then sat down in 1989 and died of "his heart" three years later.

I knew this script. Every woman in the south knows this script. You watch a man work like a mule for 40 years and then one day his body just submits the resignation he never would, and the chair takes him.

I wasn't going to sit there and watch it happen to Dale.

So I started paying attention.

And what I saw over the next 18 months scared me more than anything has ever scared me.

He stopped climbing the bigger ladders. He'd send his ground guy Reggie up instead. He covered for it. Said he wanted Reggie to "get more experience on the steeper pitches." Reggie has 22 years of experience. He didn't need more experience. Dale didn't want to climb.

He started taking naps. Dale has never napped in his life. The first time I came home and found him asleep in the chair at 3 in the afternoon I thought he was dead. I stood in the doorway and waited to see his chest move. It did. I went back outside and sat in the car for ten minutes because I didn't want him to know I'd seen him.

He started getting dizzy when he stood up too fast. He'd grab the doorframe. Hold it for a second. Then keep walking like nothing happened. I'd see him do it. He'd pretend he hadn't.

He started forgetting things. Not big things. Little things. The name of the kid who delivers our paper. Whether he'd already taken his blood pressure pill that morning. Where he set the keys.

His doctor added another medication.

Then another.

Three pills, by the end of last summer. Lisinopril. Amlodipine. Hydrochlorothiazide. A water pill that had him running to the bathroom four and five times a night and never quite catching up to himself.

The numbers came down a little. The man came down with them.

His doctor would look at the cuff and say "looking good, Dale, looking good," and Dale would smile and shake his hand and walk out to the truck holding the door frame, and we'd drive home in silence because what was there to say.

The numbers were good.

The man was disappearing.

This was the part that broke me, and I'm telling you this because I think it might be happening in your house too, and you might be telling yourself the same lies I was telling myself.

He was always tired now. Even on Sundays. He'd watch the Falcons game with his eyes closed half the time and then ask me what the score was at the end.

He stopped wanting to go to Lacey's house for Sunday supper. He'd say he was looking forward to it all week and then Sunday morning he'd come out of the bedroom and say "Janelle, I don't know if I can do it today."

He stopped going to Mancini's, which is a little hardware store on Vineville where he's been buying nails and caulk and sandpaper since 1989. Mr. Mancini's son Vincent runs it now and Vincent and Dale have a 30 year relationship that lives almost entirely inside that store. Dale stopped going because the parking lot is gravel and uneven and he didn't trust his legs on it anymore.

He stopped wanting to drive at night.

He stopped reaching for me in bed.

He stopped, basically. The man stopped.

And that Tuesday in October, when I pulled into the driveway and saw him sitting on the bottom rung of his ladder watching the sun go down, I knew the next stop on this train was the chair. The Bill Thurman chair. The Earl Conyers chair.

I knew it like I know my own name.

So I sat in the Buick and I cried, and then I got out of the car and I walked across the lawn and I said, "Dale, come inside. Reggie can finish the Hendersons tomorrow."

He looked up at me with those grey-blue eyes that I have loved for 34 years and he said, "Janelle. I'm scared."

That was the first time in 34 years of marriage I had ever heard that man use the word scared about himself.

I made him eggs that night. Two eggs over easy with grits and a slice of tomato. The supper he eats when he's sick. He ate half of it and went to bed at 8:30.

And I sat at the kitchen table and I opened my laptop, and I started reading.

This is the part of the story that changed everything, and it started because of a woman named Loretta who I'd worked with at the dental office for 11 years before she retired.

Loretta Pickens. 64. Lived out in Lizella in a house her husband Reggie built her. (Different Reggie. Macon is full of Reggies.) Her husband had been on three blood pressure medications. Sound familiar. He'd had a small stroke at 61. He'd come back about 70 percent of who he was. Loretta nursed him through it.

I called Loretta the next morning from the dental office parking lot before I went in.

"Loretta. I need to ask you something."

"Janelle, you've never called me before 9 in your life. What's wrong."

I told her about Dale on the ladder. About the chair. About the three pills and the dizziness and the man slowly disappearing inside the marriage.

She was quiet for a long time.

Then she said, "Janelle. After Reggie's stroke, I started reading. I went down a rabbit hole I didn't think I'd come back from. And I found something."

She told me about a fruit.

I almost laughed. I almost said Loretta come on now, my husband is sitting on the bottom of a ladder and you're telling me about a fruit.

But Loretta is not a foolish woman. Loretta took care of Reggie for three years after a stroke. Loretta does not chase miracle cures. So I shut up and I listened.

She told me about amla.

She pronounced it the way she'd learned to. AHM-la. Indian gooseberry. Something used in Ayurvedic medicine in India for thousands of years. She said her hairdresser, a woman named Priya, had given her the name of it after Loretta confided in her one day about Reggie.

And then Loretta said the thing that made me sit up straight in the front seat of my Buick.

"It doesn't work like the pills work, Janelle. It works on the wall."

The wall.

I asked her what she meant.

She said, "The inside of your blood vessels has a wall. A living wall. And that wall is the thing that tells your vessels to relax. When the wall is worn down, the vessels stay tight, and the pressure climbs, and the pills just force the vessels open while the wall keeps deteriorating underneath. That's why Reggie kept needing another pill, and then another, and then another. The pills weren't fixing anything. They were just managing a wall that nobody was supporting."

I sat in that parking lot and I listened to a 64 year old retired dental hygienist explain endothelial function to me, and the world rearranged itself in my head.

I went home that night and I read for four hours.

I want to tell you what I found, because I'm not a doctor and I'm not a scientist and I needed it explained the way Loretta explained it to me. In English.

Here's what's going on.

The inside of every blood vessel in your body is lined with a single layer of cells called the endothelium. People call it the wall. The wall is alive. It's not just a pipe. It's a working tissue, and one of its main jobs is to produce a molecule called nitric oxide.

Nitric oxide is what tells your blood vessels to relax. To open up. To let blood flow through without resistance.

When you're young, the wall is healthy, and it produces plenty of nitric oxide, and your vessels stay open and flexible, and your blood pressure is what it should be.

But years of inflammation, oxidative stress, processed food, sleep loss, work stress, sun exposure, age, all of it, wears that wall down. The wall stops producing as much nitric oxide as it used to. The vessels stay tight. Blood has to force its way through. The pressure climbs.

And here's the part nobody told us.

The harder blood pushes through, the more stress it puts on the wall. Which makes the wall worse. Which makes nitric oxide production drop more. Which makes the pressure climb higher.

It's a cycle that feeds itself.

That's why Dale was on three pills. That's why Loretta's Reggie was on three pills. That's why almost every man in our neighborhood over 60 is on three pills.

The pills don't get to the wall. The pills force the vessels open from the outside while the wall keeps deteriorating underneath. That's why doctors keep adding pills. The wall is going downhill and the pills are running a marathon to keep up with it.

That's what I read on a Tuesday night with my husband asleep in the next room.

And then I read about amla.

Amla contains compounds called emblicanins. These compounds have been studied for their ability to support the endothelial wall. Not force the vessels open. Support the wall itself. So the wall can do what it was built to do, which is produce its own nitric oxide.

Researchers have looked at amla in human trials for things like endothelial function, oxidative stress, and blood pressure markers. The work points in the same direction. When the wall is better supported, the body's own ability to relax its vessels can start coming back. The pressure can ease, not because something forced it down, but because the body remembers how to do what it used to do on its own.

That sentence is the one that hit me in the chest.

The body remembers how to do what it used to do on its own.

Because Dale's body remembered. I knew Dale's body remembered. I'd watched it for 34 years. The man who climbed 32 foot ladders and ate ribs at midnight and could go 14 hours on a roof in August. That man was still in there. He hadn't gone anywhere. The wall had just gotten so worn down that his body couldn't do what it knew how to do.

I ordered Alevia's Amla that night. I found it at alevia.com/amla/33. I bought two bottles because Loretta told me to. She said when you find something that supports the body the way it actually works, you don't want to be the woman waiting on a backorder.

It came Friday.

I want to tell you what happened, and I want to be honest about it because I'm not in the business of selling fairy tales. I'm a 57 year old woman with a dental office job and a husband I love. I'm not paid to lie to you.

Week one, I noticed Dale slept differently. He wasn't getting up four times a night anymore. (He's still on his pills. He didn't change those. He still talks to his doctor about those.) The bathroom trips dropped. Then I noticed he wasn't grabbing the doorframe in the morning. He'd just stand up. Walk to the kitchen. Pour the coffee.

Week two, he asked me on a Sunday if I wanted to drive out to Forsyth and look at the leaves changing.

He hadn't asked me to do anything spontaneous in two years.

Week three, I came home from work and the lawn was mowed. He'd done it himself. The man hadn't pushed a mower in 14 months. He said it just felt like a thing he could do, and so he did it.

Week four, he climbed the ladder on a job in Bonaire. Not all the way to the peak. About halfway. But he climbed it. Reggie told me about it on the phone because Dale didn't tell me. He didn't tell me because he didn't want to make a big deal of it. He just wanted to climb the ladder.

Week six was the morning I caught him standing at the kitchen window looking out at the backyard with a cup of coffee in his hand, and he turned to me and he said, "Janelle. Do you remember when we used to have people over for the Fourth of July? Maybe we should do that again next year."

I had to turn away because I didn't want him to see me cry.

That man had not talked about next year in 18 months. He'd been living in the present tense of a man who didn't think there was a next year for him.

Now let me tell you what I think is happening, because I want you to understand it the way I came to understand it.

The pills are doing what the pills do. They're forcing the vessels open. They're keeping the numbers manageable. They're a tool. Dale's doctor hasn't taken him off any of them and I'm not telling you to take your person off any of theirs. That's between you and your doctor.

But the pills were never going to support the wall.

The wall is the thing that decides whether your body can do this on its own or not.

And when the wall is supported, the cycle that was feeding itself starts breaking. The vessels relax a little more on their own. The blood doesn't push as hard. The wall isn't under the same stress. It can start functioning more like it used to.

You don't feel that on the first day. You feel it about week two. It shows up as energy. As the willingness to drive out to look at leaves. As mowing the lawn. As reaching for your wife in bed on a Sunday morning. As climbing the ladder.

It shows up as the man coming back.

Now I want to talk to you for a minute, woman to woman, because I think you might be in this story.

I think you might have a Dale.

Maybe your Dale is a roofer. Maybe he's a mechanic. Maybe he's an HVAC guy or a long haul driver or a high school football coach or a retired postal carrier or the guy who runs the meat counter at the Piggly Wiggly.

Maybe your Dale has been on two or three pills for years. Maybe his numbers are "fine." Maybe his doctor keeps saying "looking good, looking good" while your husband holds the doorframe to keep from falling over.

Maybe your Dale isn't reaching for you anymore.

Maybe your Dale is sitting on the bottom rung of his own ladder.

I want you to know something. The man is still in there. He hasn't gone anywhere. The wall has just gotten worn down, and his body has stopped being able to do what it knew how to do.

You can support the wall.

That's it.

That's the whole thing.

You support the wall, and the body remembers, and the man comes back. Not because anything got forced. Because his body did what bodies are designed to do when they finally get the right kind of help.

Alevia's Amla is the only thing I've found that works on the wall instead of around it. It's a concentrated extract of the Indian gooseberry, made in the USA, two capsules a day. 30 day bottle. They have a 90 day money-back guarantee, which means if you give it 90 days and you don't see your person come back the way Dale came back, you send it back and you get your money. Every penny.

Think about that for a minute.

Dale has been on lisinopril for 8 years. Nobody offered me a refund when he started holding the doorframe. Nobody offered me a refund when he stopped climbing his own ladder. Nobody offered me a refund for the 18 months I sat in my car and cried before I figured out what was happening.

The pharmaceutical system doesn't do refunds.

Alevia does.

That tells you something about who's confident in their product and who's just confident in their subscription.

If you've been watching the man you love disappear inside a body that used to be his, and you've been told the numbers are "fine" while you watch him fade, please. Go to the website. Read it for yourself.

You're risking nothing.

He's already risking everything.

P.S. You'll know it's working before any blood pressure cuff confirms it. With Dale, it was the bathroom trips first. Then the doorframe. Then the lawn. Then the ladder. Your body tells you the wall is doing better long before any machine does. If you've been watching your husband slow down on three pills, you've gotten used to it. You've adjusted. You've lowered the bar so gradually you've forgotten where it used to be. When it starts coming back, even a little, you'll notice. Because you'll remember who he was. And you'll realize he's still in there.

P.P.S. Alevia's Amla has a 90 day money-back guarantee. If you don't see your person come back, every penny back. No questions. The drugs Dale has been on for 8 years don't come with a money-back guarantee. Neither does the next one his doctor will prescribe. Think about what that tells you about confidence.

P.P.P.S. Alevia is a small American company. They've sold out before. Loretta told me she had to wait 11 days the second time she reordered for Reggie. I keep two bottles in the house now. One for Dale, one as a backup. Because I'm not going back to the Buick. I'm not going back to the bottom of the ladder. If your husband's next checkup is in 30 or 60 days, and you want him walking into that office with a body that's actually coming back instead of a chart that's been quietly managed for a decade, go now. They currently have a buy 3 get 2 free deal that I used to stock up. Every day the wall keeps deteriorating is another day the cycle keeps spinning. Dale waited 18 months. headline: If you own a flat-faced dog, please read this..
body:
Three French Bulldogs died at Animal Care Veterinary Clinic last month.

Same symptoms. Same age range. Same emergency presentation.

And all three owners had cooling products sitting at home.

Dr. Park noticed it first. He pulled me and Dr. Chen into his office and spread three files on his desk.

"Look at the home cooling setup."

Every single dog. Gel mat in the living room. AC running. Never went outside that day.

Every single dog. Owner reported tile behavior for months before the crisis.

Every single dog. Vet had told them the tile was normal.

Dr. Chen leaned back in her chair. "What if we've been missing something?"

That question led to a six month investigation that uncovered something the pet cooling industry has been hiding from veterinarians and flat-faced breed owners for decades.

My name is Dr. Marcus Smith, DVM. Along with Dr. James Park and Dr. Sarah Chen, we've collectively treated over 34,000 animals at Animal Care Veterinary Clinic.

And here's what we found.

It started with a French Bulldog named Bruno.

His owner, Sarah, had spent $3,400 on emergency vet bills across two summers. Bought five different cooling products. Watched him struggle through every July and August.

Everything seemed fine until the Tuesday afternoon she found him on the kitchen tile not moving.

Bruno was four years old. Perfect weight. Climate controlled apartment. AC never above 72 degrees.

She had done everything right.

Gel mat in the living room. Fan pointed at it. Walks only before 7 AM.

But Bruno kept leaving the mat after an hour and pressing his face against the kitchen tile. Every afternoon. Like clockwork.

She had posted about it in her French Bulldog group. Eleven people said Frenchies just love tile. Her vet said some dogs just prefer hard surfaces.

So she ignored it.

Then she bought a cooling vest. $67. Dried out in 40 minutes. Bruno went back to the tile.

She bought a premium gel mat. $58. Bruno stayed on it for 90 minutes. Then went to the tile.

She bought another vest from a different brand. $79. Same result. Same tile.

On that Tuesday in July Sarah was on a work call in the next room. Bruno was on the tile. She assumed he was cooling off.

When she came out twenty minutes later he wasn't moving.

His body temperature when she arrived at our clinic was 107.4 degrees. Respiratory rate 68 breaths per minute. Full heat stroke.

We tried everything. Cold water immersion. IV fluids. Oxygen.

Bruno died at 3:47 PM.

Sarah sat in our exam room afterward staring at the wall.

"I had five cooling products at home. The AC was running. He never went outside. How did this happen."

We didn't have an answer for her.

That night Dr. Park pulled every brachycephalic heat emergency file from the previous three years.

He called me and Dr. Chen in the next morning and spread them across his desk.

"Look at the home cooling setup on every single one."

We looked.

147 cases.

Every single owner had at least one cooling product at home.

138 of those dogs had never been outside on the day of their emergency.

94%.

Inside dogs. Climate controlled homes. Cooling mats in the living room.

Dying of heat stroke anyway.

Dr. Chen looked up from the files.

"We've been telling these owners their products were enough. We were wrong."

None of us said anything for a long time.

Because we had all been the vet who said the tile was normal.

We had all been the vet who said keep the AC on and you have nothing to worry about.

We had all been the vet who recommended the gel mat everyone recommends.

And 138 of those emergencies had happened to owners following our advice exactly.

We spent the next three months pulling every study on brachycephalic thermoregulation we could find. Veterinary research on airway anatomy. Comparative studies on heat emergency presentations across breeds. Biomechanical data on respiratory effort and metabolic heat generation.

Dr. Park handled the research. Dr. Chen compiled the data. I followed up with every case.

What we found contradicted everything we had been telling owners for decades combined.

Flat-faced breeds begin experiencing dangerous heat stress at just 70-71 degrees Fahrenheit. Not 90 degrees like normal dogs. Seventy-one degrees.

A climate controlled home running at 72 degrees is already above the threshold where a French Bulldog begins accumulating dangerous heat stress.

Over 90% of French Bulldogs have measurable airway restriction significant enough to directly impair their ability to regulate temperature.

But the most important discovery wasn't in the temperature data.

It was in what happens inside a flat-faced dog's body when it tries to cool itself.

Dogs cool themselves almost entirely through panting. Approximately 70% of a dog's body heat leaves through moisture evaporating from the tongue, nasal passages, and lungs during respiration.

Panting isn't just breathing. It is their entire cooling system.

But French Bulldogs, Pugs, English Bulldogs, and Boxers have Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome — BOAS. Nostrils so narrow they restrict incoming airflow. A soft palate that obstructs the airway. A windpipe congenitally undersized for the dog's body.

When a flat-faced dog pants harder to cool down, the muscular effort of forcing air through those compressed passages generates metabolic heat.

So the harder they pant, the more heat their body produces.

The more heat produced, the harder they pant.

The harder they pant, the hotter they get.

We call this the Broken Panting Loop.

Without something breaking it from the outside the loop accelerates toward crisis.

Inside. In a climate controlled home. On a cooling mat.

And here is what made all three of us furious when we finally understood it.

Every single cooling product currently marketed for flat-faced breeds was designed as if this loop doesn't exist.

Every mat. Every vest. Every elevated bed. Every fan.

All of them built on one catastrophically wrong assumption. That your dog's panting can finish the job.

After identifying the Broken Panting Loop we started asking every owner who came in with a brachycephalic breed the same questions.

"What cooling products do you use. How long does your dog stay on them. Where does your dog go when they get off."

The answers were devastating.

"He uses the mat for about an hour then goes to the tile. I thought he just liked it there."

"She gets off the mat every afternoon and lies on the bathroom floor. I figured she was comfortable."

"He pants heavily in the evenings even with the AC on. Our vet said flat-faced breeds just breathe loud."

"I've tried four different mats. Nothing keeps her settled for more than an hour."

Every single one of these owners thought they were doing enough.

Every single one had been told by a vet, a Facebook group, or a breeder that what they were seeing was normal.

And every single one of their dogs was in the Broken Panting Loop every afternoon.

Inside. With the AC running.

If your flat-faced dog shows ANY of these signs, the Broken Panting Loop is likely already active:

Leaves their cooling mat after 60-90 minutes and moves to the tile or bathroom floor. Seems warm even with the AC running. Pants heavily in the afternoon even indoors. Restless during peak heat hours despite the AC being on. Breathing sounds more labored in the evening than the morning.

These aren't quirks. These aren't preferences.

These are the only ways your dog can tell you the cooling product stopped working.

After discovering how widespread the warning signs were we thought the hard part was over.

Just find a product built around the correct assumption. Not assist the panting. Replace it entirely.

We were wrong.

Dr. Chen took the lead on this. She ordered every cooling product on the market claiming extended duration or brachycephalic specific design. Twelve products. All claiming to work longer. All claiming to be built for flat-faced breeds.

She tested each one on brachycephalic patients across our three practices using the same protocol. Tracking cooling duration, respiratory rate changes, and behavior at the point of product failure.

What came back made all three of us furious.

Standard gel mat. Bestseller on Amazon. 4.5 stars. Thousands of reviews. Effective for 60-90 minutes before equilibrating to body temperature. Hands off to the Broken Panting Loop. Dog goes to the tile.

Premium gel mat. $58. Marketed specifically for brachycephalic breeds. Same gel technology underneath. Same 75-90 minute failure window. Same tile.

Cooling vest. $67-$89. Sold everywhere. Designed for evaporative cooling. Requires functional panting. Dries out in 35-45 minutes. Becomes damp fabric around a dog whose evaporative system was never going to work. Dog goes to the tile.

Elevated mesh bed. Marketed to increase airflow. Requires functional panting. Increasing airflow around a broken evaporative system does nothing. Dog goes to the tile.

Extended duration gel mat. Premium priced. Claims 4 hours of cooling. Same gel base as every other product. Equilibrates to body temperature within 90 minutes regardless of thickness. Dog goes to the tile.

Cooling pad with built in ice pockets. Effective for 45 minutes until the ice melts. Hands off to the Broken Panting Loop. Dog goes to the tile.

Every product. Same wrong assumption. Same failure point. Same tile.

We had been recommending products to owners that were all failing at the same point for the same reason.

All three of us felt like we had failed them.

The pet cooling industry is almost completely unregulated for efficacy claims. Companies can put a French Bulldog on the packaging and claim it works for flat-faced breeds. Nobody verifies actual cooling duration under real conditions. Nobody tests whether the product accounts for the Broken Panting Loop.

Dr. Park spent two months contacting manufacturers. Demanding independent thermal testing data. Duration documentation under real ambient conditions. Breed specific efficacy studies.

Most wouldn't respond. Those who did sent marketing materials not data.

We were about to give up.

Then Dr. Park found the Umphora CoolBreath™ Mat.

All three of us were skeptical. Another company making big promises.

But when Dr. Park called something was different.

They answered. Immediately.

He asked for their thermal testing data. They emailed it within the hour from an accredited independent third party lab.

He asked about the technology. Phase change material. Not gel. Not water. A material engineered to absorb heat through a sustained molecular process. The material changes from solid to liquid at a precisely engineered temperature and during that transition it absorbs enormous amounts of thermal energy without increasing in temperature itself.

He asked for duration testing under real ambient conditions. Six months of batch testing results. Sustained cooling for 4-6 hours at 75 degrees ambient temperature. Not 90 minutes. Four to six hours.

He asked how they addressed the Broken Panting Loop specifically. They sent their design brief. The material density, thermal absorption rate, and phase transition temperature were all engineered around one specific assumption.

That the dog lying on this mat cannot cool itself from the inside and the mat has to do the entire job continuously for hours.

They were the only company that could answer every single question.

Dr. Chen sent their product to the same independent lab she had used for the others.

The results:

Sustained cooling at consistent temperature for 4-6 hours at 75 degrees ambient. Every other product failed within 90 minutes under the same conditions. Zero degradation in thermal performance across repeated use cycles. Phase change material verified as engineered specifically for brachycephalic thermoregulation failure.

All three of us read the results separately.

We reached the same conclusion independently.

This was the only cooling product built around the correct assumption.

I called Sarah that same week.

"We found something. It won't bring Bruno back. But I need you to know it exists."

Six months later Sarah got a new French Bulldog. A two year old named Reggie.

She put the CoolBreath™ Mat down the first day she brought him home.

Week one. Reggie stayed on the mat for three hours without going to the tile.

Week two. His breathing during afternoon rest was quieter than she had ever heard a French Bulldog breathe in summer.

Week three. She called me.

"Dr. Smith. The AC is set to 72 like always. But he's just lying on the mat. Calm. Quiet. Not going to the tile. I didn't know this was possible."

It was possible.

Because his body finally had something that did the cooling his panting never could.

We've now recommended the CoolBreath™ Mat to every brachycephalic breed owner who comes through our clinic.

Across our three practices over 14 months we tracked outcomes from 347 owners.

91% reported visible reduction in respiratory effort during peak heat hours within the first two weeks.

96% reported their dog staying on the mat significantly longer than any previous cooling product.

Zero repeat heat emergency presentations in dogs whose owners were using the mat consistently.

Before we started recommending it we were seeing a combined average of 18-22 brachycephalic heat emergencies per summer from owners we had previously treated.

Last summer across all three practices we saw four.

Sarah, French Bulldog owner, lost Bruno to indoor heat stroke:

"Bruno never went outside that day. The AC was on. He had his mat. I did everything I was told to do. When the doctors explained the Broken Panting Loop I finally understood why none of it was enough. Reggie has been on the CoolBreath™ Mat for eight months. He has never once gone to the tile."

James, English Bulldog owner, two indoor heat emergencies in three years:

"Both times Tank never left the house. Both times the AC was running. My vet said he just runs hot. He still ended up in emergency twice. Bills were over $4,000 combined. Four months on the CoolBreath™ Mat. No emergencies. His vet commented on how much quieter his breathing has been."

Amanda, French Bulldog owner, five cooling products across two summers:

"I had spent $271 on products that all stopped working after an hour. The difference was immediate. She stays on it. She doesn't go to the tile. She just settles. That has never happened with anything else I've bought."

These aren't miracles.

This is simply what happens when you give a flat-faced dog's body what it actually needs. Something that does the cooling their body cannot do. For long enough to actually matter. Built for their specific biology.

Here's what we need you to understand.

Keeping your dog inside is not a cooling strategy.

The AC is not a cooling strategy.

They are the minimum baseline. Not the solution.

A French Bulldog in a 72 degree home with a cooling mat that stops working after 90 minutes is in the Broken Panting Loop every single afternoon.

Inside. Safe looking. In crisis.

147 files taught us that.

Don't keep buying different versions of the same product built on the same wrong assumption.

Don't spend another summer thinking the AC is enough.

Don't wait for a midnight emergency room call to find out your mat wasn't built for your dog.

We tested twelve products across three practices. Every single one failed at the same point for the same reason. Because every single one was built for a dog whose panting works.

Your dog's panting does not work that way.

We could have stayed quiet. Kept telling owners to keep their dogs inside. Kept watching the same emergencies come through our doors every summer from owners who had done exactly what we told them.

But we became veterinarians to help animals. Not to give advice that leaves owners standing in an exam room asking a question we couldn't answer.

How did this happen. He never went outside.

Now we have an answer.

And so do you.

The only cooling product all three of us recommend to every flat-faced breed owner who comes through our clinic after testing everything on the market:

https://tryumphora.com/products/coolbreath-mat

P.S. 94% of the flat-faced breeds we treated for heat stroke had never been outside that day. They were inside. With the AC running. On a cooling mat that stopped working 90 minutes after they lay down. Keeping your dog indoors is not enough on its own. The Broken Panting Loop doesn't care about your thermostat. The only thing that breaks it is sustained external cooling built for a dog whose panting can't finish the job. Don't find out the hard way that everything else you tried was built for a different dog.

P.P.S. None of us get paid by Umphora to recommend this. We recommend it because after testing twelve cooling products across 347 brachycephalic patients it was the only one that passed independent thermal testing and the only one built around the correct assumption. After our combined 40 years and 34,000 plus animals treated at Animal Care Veterinary Clinic we finally have an answer to the question we could never answer before. Your dog deserves that.

headline: Feel Like Yourself Again
body:
I almost didn't go to my granddaughter's piano recital.

She's 7 years old. Her name is Amaya. She has been practicing the same Beethoven piece for 4 months on the upright in her mother's living room, the one with the sticky middle C, and she was going to play it in front of 60 people in the auditorium at Springhill Elementary on a Thursday night in March, and her grandmother almost stayed home.

I sat in my bedroom in my best blue dress with my purse on my lap and I told my husband Cornell to go on without me. He turned around in the doorway with his tie half knotted and he said, "Loretta. What are you doing."

I said, "My pressure is up. I can feel it. I don't want to make a scene."

He looked at me for a long time. Then he said, "Loretta. She's going to look for you in that crowd."

And I sat on the edge of the bed and I cried.

Because I was 63 years old and I was scared to sit in an auditorium full of people for 90 minutes because I was afraid my body was going to embarrass me in front of my own grandbaby.

My name is Loretta. I live in Mobile, Alabama, off Spring Hill Avenue, in the same little brick house Cornell and I bought in 1991 when our oldest was 4. I worked 31 years as a kindergarten teacher in the Mobile County school system. I retired in 2022. I have three children and 6 grandchildren and the youngest of them, Amaya, is the one who calls me on FaceTime every Sunday after church to show me her piano hands.

And on a Thursday night in March 2024, I almost missed her first recital because my blood pressure had been climbing for two years and the pills my doctor gave me weren't holding it down anymore.

Let me back up.

I was diagnosed with high blood pressure when I was 54. Doctor Whitfield at the clinic on Airport Boulevard, the one with the parking lot you can never find a spot in, he took my pressure twice on a Tuesday morning and he said, "Mrs. Loretta, your top number is 162. We need to put you on something."

I didn't argue. My mama died of a stroke at 71. My daddy had two heart attacks before he was 65. I knew what runs in our family. I took the lisinopril and I started walking three mornings a week and I cut back on the salt and I thought I was doing what I was supposed to do.

For a few years, it held. Numbers came down. Not great, but better. The doctor was happy.

Then about two years ago, somewhere around 61, it started creeping back. He upped the lisinopril. Added a water pill. The numbers came down for a minute and then crept back. He added a third medication, a calcium channel blocker, and the side effects started. My ankles swelled up like sausages by 4 in the afternoon. My head felt thick. I started getting dizzy when I stood up from the couch.

I'd go for my checkups and the cuff would beep and Cornell would be sitting in the chair across from me and I'd watch his face change when the numbers came up on the little screen. 156 over 94. 162 over 98. 168 over 101.

Doctor Whitfield would shake his head and adjust the prescription and say, "Loretta, sometimes the body just doesn't want to cooperate. We'll keep working on it."

Keep working on it. Like my pressure was a stubborn child in his classroom.

But the thing nobody talks about with blood pressure is what it does to you between the appointments. Not the numbers. The fear.

I started feeling it in my chest at random times. A heaviness. A pressure behind my eyes when I bent down to tie my shoes. I'd be at the grocery store reaching for a can of crushed tomatoes on the top shelf and a wave of something would come over me and I'd have to grab the cart handle and wait for it to pass.

I stopped going to the Thursday night Bible study at Mount Olive because the church is on the third floor of the family life center and the elevator is slow and I started worrying about getting stuck in it.

I stopped going to my sister Vernita's apartment because it's two flights up and by the time I got to her door I was breathing like I'd run a mile.

I started saying no to things. Not big things, at first. Just little things. The Saturday cookout at my brother in law's. The hair appointment with Miss Yvonne who I'd been seeing since 1998. My friend Patrice's birthday dinner at Wintzell's downtown.

I told everyone I was just tired. Cornell knew. He knew because he'd come home and find me sitting in the recliner in the dark with the TV off, just sitting there, listening to my own heart.

And then Amaya's recital came up.

My daughter Tanya called me three weeks ahead to remind me. She'd already called me two times before that. She knew, the way daughters know, that I'd been pulling back. She said, "Mama. Amaya is asking when you're coming. She wants to know what color dress you're wearing so she can match her ribbon."

I told her I'd be there. I bought the blue dress at Dillard's that weekend. I had my hair done on the Wednesday before. I planned it out the way you plan out a trip to a foreign country, every step, every parking lot, every bathroom, every chair.

And the morning of the recital my pressure cuff at home read 171 over 103 and I sat down on the edge of the tub in my bathroom and I thought, "I cannot do this. I cannot sit in that crowd for 90 minutes with my pressure like this. What if something happens? In front of all those people? In front of Amaya?"

That's why I was sitting on the edge of the bed with my purse on my lap.

Cornell stood in the doorway with his tie half knotted and he didn't say anything for a minute. Then he came over and he sat down next to me and he put his hand on my back and he said, "Loretta. We're going. I'll drive slow. You sit in the back row by the door. If you need to step out, you step out. But she's looking for you. We're going."

I went. I sat in the back row. I held Cornell's hand so hard I left half moons in his palm with my fingernails. Amaya played her Beethoven and she missed two notes and she kept going and at the end she stood up and bowed and she looked into that crowd and she found me. She found me in the back row. And she waved. A little wave. Just for her grandmama.

I cried in the parking lot. I cried in the car. I cried in the driveway when we got home.

Because I had spent 90 minutes scared. I had spent 90 minutes with my hand on my chest. I had spent 90 minutes calculating how fast I could get to the exit. And my granddaughter, my baby, had stood on that stage and looked for me and I should have been the proudest woman in that room and instead I was the most afraid.

That night, after Cornell went to bed, I sat at the kitchen table in my robe and I opened up the laptop and I started looking.

I had spent two years doing what my doctor told me to do. Three medications. Low salt. Walking. Weight down 12 pounds. And my numbers were worse than the day I started. And I had just sat through my grandbaby's recital with my hand on my chest.

I wasn't looking to replace the medication. I wasn't trying to be one of those people who throws away her pills and trusts the universe. I was a kindergarten teacher. I believe in science. But I was looking for what my doctor wasn't telling me.

I typed in things like "why isn't my blood pressure medication working" and "what doctors don't explain about hypertension." I went down a rabbit hole that started on a regular health website and ended somewhere I didn't expect.

I found an article about the inside of your blood vessels.

I didn't know your blood vessels had an inside. I mean, I knew they were tubes, but I didn't know there was a living layer on the inside of those tubes that does work. It's called the endothelium, and it's not just a wall. It's a living membrane that controls whether your blood vessels stay tight or whether they relax. It does this by producing a molecule called nitric oxide. When the endothelium is healthy and functioning properly, it produces plenty of nitric oxide, your vessels relax, blood flows freely, and your pressure stays where it ought to be.

But years of inflammation, stress, processed food, and just plain getting older wear that inner wall down. When the wall isn't functioning properly, it can't produce as much nitric oxide. So your vessels stay tight. Blood forces its way through. That's high blood pressure. And the harder the blood pushes through tight vessels, the more it stresses the wall, which makes it worse, which raises the pressure more. A cycle that feeds itself.

I sat at the kitchen table reading that and I felt something I hadn't felt in two years.

I felt like somebody was finally telling me what was wrong with me.

Because every pill I'd been taking, the lisinopril, the water pill, the calcium channel blocker, they all force your vessels open from the outside or pull fluid out of you. None of them touch the wall. The wall keeps deteriorating underneath. Which is why the medications stopped working over time. Which is why my doctor kept "adjusting" them. Which is why I was sitting in the back row at my granddaughter's recital scared.

The article mentioned a few things that had been studied for supporting the function of that inner wall. Most of them were ingredients I'd never heard of. But one of them I had heard of.

Amla.

A little green fruit from India. Indian gooseberry. They've been using it for thousands of years in Ayurvedic medicine. The article said it contains compounds called emblicanins that have been studied for their role in supporting endothelial function, the function of that inner wall. When the wall is supported and functioning better, it can start producing more of its own nitric oxide, the way it did when you were younger. Vessels can relax more naturally. Pressure on the walls can ease.

It wasn't a drug. It wasn't forcing anything. It was supporting the part of your body that's supposed to regulate your pressure in the first place.

I want to be careful here. I'm not telling you I read one article and ran out and changed my whole life. I read articles for three weeks. I read about the research. I read about the studies. I read about what amla was and what it wasn't. I called my niece Brittany, who is a nurse practitioner in Atlanta, and I asked her what she thought. She said she'd been recommending amla to some of her patients for over a year as part of a broader plan. She said the research on the vascular side was real, even if it wasn't going to make the evening news.

She said something I'll never forget. She said, "Aunt Loretta, the medications you're taking force your vessels open. They don't fix the reason your vessels are tight in the first place. Amla works further upstream. It supports the wall itself. That's a different conversation."

A different conversation.

That's what I'd been wanting for two years. A different conversation.

I ordered a bottle from Alevia that night. It was 4 days before it arrived. Brown box on the porch when I came back from the mailbox.

I told Cornell I was going to try something. I didn't make a big speech about it. I just told him. He looked at the bottle. He read the label. He said, "Loretta, you're a grown woman. You do what you think is right."

I started taking two capsules every morning with my coffee. I did not stop my medications. I did not change anything else. I just added the amla and I kept doing what I was doing. I told my niece and she told me to track my numbers every morning so I'd have something to look at.

The first week, nothing changed. I expected nothing. I'd been on lisinopril for 9 years. I knew bodies don't turn on a dime.

The second week, my morning numbers came down a few points. Could've been anything. I wrote it down.

The third week, my ankles weren't as swollen at the end of the day. I noticed it when I was putting on my house slippers. The little dent the elastic leaves in my ankle was lighter.

The fourth week, I walked from my car to the grocery store entrance at Winn Dixie and I didn't have to stop to catch my breath halfway. I stood in the produce section and I picked up a cantaloupe and I realized, in that moment, that I had stopped doing this calculation in my head. The calculation of how far I could walk before I had to rest. The calculation of where the nearest place to sit was. I had been doing that calculation, all day, every day, for almost two years, and that afternoon at Winn Dixie I had walked all the way to the produce section without doing it.

I bought the cantaloupe. I bought some greens. I walked back to my car. And I sat in the driver's seat in the parking lot and I called Cornell.

He said, "Loretta. You okay?"

I said, "Cornell. I'm good. I'm good."

By the sixth week, my morning numbers were sitting in the 140s over the 80s on a consistent basis. Not the 160s. The 140s. That's the lowest they'd been in 18 months. I went to my appointment with Doctor Whitfield in the eighth week and the cuff beeped and the screen read 142 over 86 and he looked at the screen and then he looked at me.

"Loretta," he said. "What are you doing?"

I told him. He listened. He didn't dismiss me, to his credit, but he didn't get excited either. He said, "Well, whatever you're doing, keep doing it, and let's see what your numbers look like in another two months."

In another two months they were better.

He took me off the water pill. He cut the calcium channel blocker in half. I went from three medications to one and a half, and my pressure stayed in the 130s and 140s. My ankles deflated. My head cleared up. The thickness behind my eyes went away. The pressure in my chest that I'd been carrying around like a sandbag, it stopped showing up.

But I'm going to tell you about the moment that mattered most.

It was Amaya's birthday party at the Pizza Hut on Government Boulevard in July. She turned 8. Tanya had rented the back room. There were 14 kids running around screaming. Pizza grease everywhere. The party hat elastic was cutting into my chin. The kids were doing the limbo. They put on that little plastic stick across two chairs.

Amaya looked at me and she said, "Grandmama, you go."

A year before, I would have said no. A year before, I would have been sitting in the corner with my hand on my chest counting the minutes until the cake came out so I could leave.

I got up. I walked over to the limbo stick. The kids were laughing. Cornell was filming on his phone.

I bent backwards. I went under that stick. I did not feel my chest. I did not feel my pressure. I felt my back complaining a little and my knees telling me I was 64, but I did not feel scared. I went under and I came up the other side and Amaya hollered like she'd just won the lottery and she ran over and hugged my legs and I picked her up.

I picked her up.

I had not picked up a grandchild in 2 years.

She put her face into my neck and I just held her there. And I thought about a woman who almost didn't go to a piano recital because she was scared of her own body in a crowded room. And I thought about how that woman was gone now. How somewhere between the night I sat at the kitchen table reading about endothelial function and the afternoon at Pizza Hut, that woman had walked back into her life.

Let me say plainly what I think happened.

The medication my doctor gave me was doing one job. It was forcing my vessels to relax from the outside. It was not addressing why my vessels weren't relaxing on their own anymore. The inner wall had been deteriorating for years and nothing in any of the pills I was taking was supporting that wall. So the underlying problem kept getting worse, even as the medication held the numbers down for a while.

Adding amla supported the wall. The wall could start producing more of its own nitric oxide. My vessels could start relaxing more naturally. The cycle that had been feeding itself for years could start to ease. Pressure could come down, not because something was forcing it, but because the part of my body that was supposed to regulate it had finally been given some support.

I'm not throwing my medication away. I'm still on a small dose of the lisinopril and I'm still walking three mornings a week and I'm still watching what I eat. But the difference between where I was a year ago and where I am now is the difference between sitting in the back row of an auditorium with my hand on my chest, and bending backwards under a limbo stick at my grandbaby's birthday party.

That difference has a name. The name is Alevia Amla.

I order it directly from their website. The bottle is honest. The dosing is clean. They back it with a 90 day money back guarantee, which means if you try it for three months and your body responds the way it did before you started, you send the empty bottle back and they refund every dollar. No questions. No phone tree. No fight.

That guarantee is the reason I tried it in the first place. I was a kindergarten teacher who lived on a budget. I wasn't going to throw away money on something that might not do anything. The 90 days meant I could find out for myself, with no risk.

If you've been watching your numbers creep, and you've been adding medications, and you've been told by a kind doctor that "sometimes the body just doesn't cooperate," I want to tell you that there is another conversation happening. It's not the medication conversation. It's the wall conversation. And it might be the conversation that matters most.

Amaya is going to have another recital in May. She's playing a Chopin piece this time. I am going to be in the third row, center aisle, with a clear view of her hands.

I am not going to be afraid.

P.S. The 90 day guarantee is the part I want you to really hear. I almost didn't try amla because I thought I was just going to waste 40 dollars. Alevia stands behind the bottle. If it doesn't do anything for you in three months, you get every penny back. There is no risk in finding out.

P.P.S. I'm not telling you to stop your medications. I didn't stop mine. I added something that supported what my medications couldn't reach. Talk to your doctor. Track your numbers. Pay attention to your own body. But please don't keep sitting in the back row.

P.P.P.S. Amaya asked me last Sunday on FaceTime what color dress I was wearing to the May recital. I told her I hadn't decided. She said, "Grandmama, wear the blue one. I like that one." I'm wearing the blue one.
